title: Amos 8
subtitle: Summer Fruit: The End Has Come

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

sections.header: Verses 1-6 — The Fourth Vision and the Marketplace

heb.paragraph: The wordplay is the key to the vision: qayits (summer fruit, the last harvest before winter) sounds like qets (end, termination). God says 'The time is ripe (qets) for my people Israel; I will spare them no longer.' The pun is untranslatable but devastating: the ripe fruit signals that Israel's time has run out.

refs.note: 'Am I still to forget your ill-gotten treasures and the short ephah?' — Micah's parallel condemnation of commercial fraud.
The Torah's prohibition against dishonest weights and measures, which the merchants of Amos 8 are violating.

notes.note: The basket of ripe fruit represents Israel at the end of its season. The wordplay between qayits (summer fruit) and qets (end) delivers the message: Israel's time of prosperity has reached its terminus. Judgment is next.

The merchants trample the needy, cannot wait for the Sabbath to end so they can resume cheating, and rig their scales. They sell the refuse of grain and buy the poor for a pair of sandals. This passage reveals the moral bankruptcy of Israel's commercial class.

Calvin reads the summer fruit vision as a warning against spiritual complacency: prosperity can itself be a sign that the end is near. The ripeness that should have produced gratitude instead produced corruption.
Calvin connects economic fraud to idolatry: those who cheat the poor do not truly worship God, regardless of their Sabbath observance. Religious ritual without economic justice is hypocrisy.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew wordplay qayits/qets cannot be reproduced in English. The NIV's 'ripe' captures the metaphorical sense but loses the phonetic connection. The pun functions as a divine revelation embedded in language itself.

The merchants' complaint about the New Moon and Sabbath reveals that they observe these holy days only externally. Their hearts are in the marketplace. The specific frauds mentioned — smaller ephah (dry measure), bigger shekel (weight), and rigged scales — are precisely the practices prohibited in Deut 25:13-16.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart reads the fourth vision as the complement to the third: where the plumb line showed Israel failing the test, the summer fruit shows Israel at the end of its harvest. Both images convey finality. The singing in the temple becoming wailing (v. 3) is the auditory equivalent of the transition from feasting to mourning.

Stuart provides detailed analysis of the commercial frauds: the ephah was a dry measure of about 22 liters; making it smaller cheated the buyer. The shekel was the weight used to measure payment; making it bigger overcharged. The combination of both frauds doubled the theft. Stuart notes that these are not exceptional crimes but systemic practices embedded in the marketplace.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman connect the vision (vv. 1-3) to the indictment (vv. 4-6) through the theme of ripeness and rot: the fruit is ripe, and so is the corruption. The merchants' crimes recapitulate the charges of 2:6-8, creating a structural inclusio between the opening oracle against Israel and this penultimate vision.

hist.context: The fourth vision uses a pun to deliver its message: summer fruit (qayits) sounds like 'the end' (qets). The vision transitions into a direct indictment of commercial fraud (vv. 4-6): merchants who can't wait for the Sabbath to end so they can cheat customers with rigged scales, sell the sweepings of grain, and buy the poor for a pair of sandals. The economic critique is precise: these are not random accusations but descriptions of specific commercial practices that exploited the vulnerable.

sections.header: Verses 7-14 — Famine of the Word of the LORD

heb.paragraph: The famine of vv. 11-12 is uniquely devastating: not hunger for bread or thirst for water, but for hearing 'the words of the LORD.' God will withdraw prophetic revelation — the ultimate punishment for a people who silenced his prophets (2:12; 7:12-13). Those who rejected the word when it was offered will search for it in vain.

refs.note: 'The word of the LORD was rare; there were not many visions' — an earlier period of prophetic silence, before Samuel's call.

'Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God' — Jesus affirms the priority of God's word over physical sustenance, the principle underlying Amos' famine imagery.

notes.note: The sun going dark at noon and festivals turning to mourning describe a reversal of creation and celebration. God will turn their joy into the deepest grief, like mourning for an only son.

The famine of the word of the LORD is the most terrifying judgment in Amos. When God withdraws his word, a nation is left without guidance, correction, or hope. Those who rejected the prophets will desperately search for what they once despised.
Swearing by the 'sin of Samaria' and the gods of Dan and Beersheba identifies the idolatrous cults that have replaced YHWH worship. Those who swear by false gods will fall, never to rise.

Calvin identifies the withdrawal of God's word as the supreme judgment: worse than plague, war, or famine is the silence of God. When God stops speaking, there is no salvation, no correction, no hope. This is the punishment most suited to those who stopped their ears to prophetic preaching.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The reference to the sun going dark at noon may allude to a solar eclipse. A total eclipse occurred on June 15, 763 BC, visible in the region and recorded in Assyrian eponym lists. If Amos' audience had witnessed this eclipse, the imagery would have been terrifyingly vivid.

The 'famine ... of hearing the words of the LORD' uses the construct chain to redefine hunger: the object of deprivation is not food but divine revelation. The reversal is poignant: the people who silenced prophets (2:12; 7:13) will now search in vain for what they rejected.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart connects the cosmic signs (earthquake, darkened sun, mourning) to the Day of the LORD tradition established in 5:18-20. The mourning 'as for an only son' (v. 10) is the most bitter form of grief in Israelite culture, applied now to the entire nation's fate.

Stuart reads the famine of the word as the ultimate covenant curse: in the Deuteronomic framework, obedience brings God's presence and revelation, while disobedience brings silence. The geographical range of the search ('from sea to sea, from north to east') notably omits the south — the direction of Jerusalem — suggesting that the northern kingdom has lost access to the true sanctuary.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Andersen and Freedman argue that the famine of the word (vv. 11-12) is the theological counterpart to the physical famine of 4:6: where God sent hunger to provoke repentance and Israel refused, now God sends the worse hunger — silence — as the final consequence of that refusal. The two famines bracket the central speeches, creating a progression from warning to judgment.

hist.context: This section moves from cosmic upheaval (the land trembling, the sun going dark at noon, festivals turning to mourning) to the most devastating punishment Amos announces: the withdrawal of God's word. People will stagger from sea to sea, searching for the word of the LORD, and not find it. The famine of the word is worse than any physical famine because it removes the possibility of divine guidance and restoration. The chapter closes with the strongest of Israel finding their strength failing (v. 13) and those who swear by false gods falling, never to rise again (v. 14).

themes.note: Judgment at 10: the end has come, and the word of the LORD will be withdrawn. Prophecy at 9: the famine of the word is the climactic punishment for silencing prophets. Justice at 8: marketplace fraud is the specific charge.

rows.label: vv.1-6
rows.text: Summer Fruit Vision and Marketplace Fraud

rows.label: vv.7-14
rows.text: Cosmic Signs and the Famine of God's Word

lit.note: Chapter 8 pairs the fourth vision (summer fruit/the end) with its consequences: cosmic upheaval, the reversal of festivals, and the ultimate punishment — the withdrawal of divine revelation. The chapter moves from wordplay to marketplace to cosmos to silence.

hebtext.note: The wordplay is the key to the vision: qayits (summer fruit, the last harvest before winter) sounds like qets (end, termination). God says 'The time is ripe (qets) for my people Israel; I will spare them no longer.' The pun is untranslatable but devastating: the ripe fruit signals that Israel's time has run out.

The famine of vv. 11-12 is uniquely devastating: not hunger for bread or thirst for water, but for hearing 'the words of the LORD.' God will withdraw prophetic revelation — the ultimate punishment for a people who silenced his prophets (2:12; 7:12-13). Those who rejected the word when it was offered will search for it in vain.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Summer Fruit: The End Has Come

translations.text: saying, “When will the New Moon be over that we may sell grain, and the Sabbath be ended that we may market wheat?”— skimping on the measure, boosting the price and cheating with dishonest scales,

saying, “When will the new moon be over, that we may sell grain? And the Sabbath, that we may offer wheat for sale, that we may make the ephah small and the shekel great and deal deceitfully with false balances,
“In that day,” declares the Sovereign Lord, “the songs in the temple will turn to wailing. Many, many bodies—flung everywhere! Silence!”
The songs of the temple shall become wailings in that day,” declares the Lord GOD. “So many dead bodies!” “They are thrown everywhere!” “Silence!”

thread.text: 'Am I still to forget your ill-gotten treasures and the short ephah?' — Micah's parallel condemnation of commercial fraud.
The Torah's prohibition against dishonest weights and measures, which the merchants of Amos 8 are violating.
'The word of the LORD was rare; there were not many visions' — an earlier period of prophetic silence, before Samuel's call.

'Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God' — Jesus affirms the priority of God's word over physical sustenance, the principle underlying Amos' famine imagery.

coaching.tip: The vision of summer fruit (qayits) puns on 'the end' (qets). Israel is ripe — for judgment. 'The end has come upon my people Israel' (v. 2). The wordplay is untranslatable but devastating: summer's harvest signals not abundance but termination. Language itself carries the verdict.

'I will send a famine... of hearing the words of the LORD' (v. 11). The ultimate judgment: prophetic silence. They ignored the word when it came; now they'll crave it and find nothing. God's absence is worse than God's rebuke. Divine silence terrifies those who finally want to listen.
